company.name: Progressive
company.description: Progressive provides personal and commercial auto insurance and other property and casualty insurance products across the United States.

overview.title: Business profile
paragraphs: Progressive is one of the largest U.S. auto insurance groups, with personal auto, commercial auto, motorcycle, boat, recreational vehicle, property, workers' compensation, and other specialty property-casualty insurance lines.
The company sells through independent agencies, direct online and phone channels, and the Progressive mobile app, giving it both broad distribution and substantial first-party customer data.

overview.title: Financial position
paragraphs: Progressive reported 2024 net premiums written of $74.4 billion, net income of $8.5 billion, and a 2024 underwriting margin of 11.2%, well above its long-running 96 combined-ratio target framework.
CompaniesMarketCap reported Progressive's market capitalization at $116.41 billion in May 2026, placing it among the largest public insurance companies globally.

moatNarrative: Progressive's moat is built around actuarial data, scale, state-by-state regulatory filings, claims operations, brand recognition, agent relationships, and direct digital acquisition. In auto insurance, more policies and claims data improve pricing segmentation, while regulatory and capital requirements make fast replication difficult.

Snapshot deepens the data advantage by turning driving behavior into pricing signals, but that same telematics edge also creates a visible target for privacy-preserving, user-controlled, or cooperative insurance models.
decentralizationNarrative: Progressive's core product is hard to decentralize because licensed insurance requires capital, reserving, claims handling, fraud controls, regulatory filings, and consumer protection compliance. A credible replacement cannot simply be an app; it must coordinate risk capital, underwriting rules, claims verification, and legal accountability.

The strongest decentralization pressure is likely to come first at the edges: mutual aid pools, parametric coverage, user-owned driving data, auditable claims workflows, and federated insurance cooperatives that partner with licensed carriers or reinsurers before attempting full-stack replacement.
company.snapshotNote: Queued from the May 25, 2026 S&P 500 market-cap snapshot ranks 76-100.

moat.rationale: Progressive combines national scale, large policy and claims datasets, multichannel distribution, regulatory licenses, underwriting discipline, and a major telematics program.

decentralizability.rationale: Insurance can use decentralized coordination for data, capital pools, and claims transparency, but full replacement is constrained by regulation, solvency requirements, fraud risk, and the need for licensed claims and policy administration.

profitability.rationale: Progressive reported 2024 net income of $8.5 billion, an 11.2% underwriting margin, and 35.5% return on average common shareholders' equity.

peRatio.rationale: Approximate market-cap-to-2024-net-income ratio using CompaniesMarketCap's May 2026 market cap of $116.41 billion and Progressive's 2024 net income of $8.5 billion; this is not a live trailing-twelve-month market data feed.

marketCap.rationale: CompaniesMarketCap reported Progressive's market capitalization as $116.41 billion as of May 2026.

products.name: Progressive auto insurance
products.summary: Progressive's auto insurance franchise covers personal and commercial drivers through direct and agency channels, backed by pricing, claims, service, and regulatory infrastructure.
products.whyItMatters: Auto insurance is legally required for most drivers, so a high-scale incumbent can shape pricing, claims norms, repair workflows, and data practices for millions of households and businesses.
replacementSketch: A credible open replacement would start as a licensed mutual, reciprocal, or cooperative layer that lets members pool risks transparently while outsourcing regulated paper, reinsurance, and claims administration where required.

Over time, open rating models, portable driving data, auditable reserves, and member governance could pressure opaque pricing and claims practices without pretending that regulation and capital requirements disappear.

alternatives.name: Etherisc
alternatives.summary: Etherisc is a decentralized insurance protocol and Generic Insurance Framework for building blockchain-based insurance products with modular smart contracts, risk pools, and oracle-driven processes.

openness.rationale: Etherisc publishes protocol documentation and maintains public code repositories, though production insurance products still require implementation-specific governance and compliance choices.

decentralizationFit.rationale: The project is explicitly designed around decentralized insurance applications, smart contracts, risk pools, and oracle-based claims automation.

readiness.rationale: Etherisc provides reusable infrastructure and examples, but a Progressive-scale U.S. auto insurance replacement would still need licensing, actuarial models, claims operations, and consumer distribution.

costLeverage.rationale: Smart-contract automation and transparent pools could reduce some administrative overhead, but regulated auto insurance costs are dominated by claims, capital, repair networks, fraud controls, and compliance.

alternatives.name: Teambrella
alternatives.summary: Teambrella is a peer-to-peer insurance design in which team members jointly evaluate claims and coordinate reimbursements through shared governance and cryptocurrency-based settlement concepts.

openness.rationale: The whitepaper describes the model in detail, but the available evidence is stronger as an open design reference than as a broadly maintained open-source insurance stack.

decentralizationFit.rationale: The model is organized around peer evaluation, team governance, and distributed claim decisions rather than a single centralized insurer.

readiness.rationale: Peer-to-peer insurance concepts are useful for mutual aid and small pools, but replacing regulated auto insurance at scale would require licensing, capital adequacy, consumer protection, and mature fraud controls.

costLeverage.rationale: Member adjudication and reduced centralized administration could lower some overhead, but claim severity, legal disputes, and adverse selection limit near-term leverage.

disruptionConcepts.name: Federated mutual auto insurance pools
disruptionConcepts.summary: Member-owned insurance pools could federate across local or affinity groups, sharing open actuarial models, reserves dashboards, dispute rules, and reinsurance access while keeping underwriting governance closer to policyholders.
disruptionConcepts.thesis: Progressive's advantage depends partly on centralized underwriting, pricing opacity, and scale distribution; federated mutual pools would shift some pricing and governance power back to policyholders while using shared infrastructure for compliance and risk transfer.
disruptionConcepts.bitcoinOrDecentralizationRole: Decentralization matters through federated governance, auditable reserves, shared claims rules, and member-controlled data rather than through Bitcoin payments. The economic role is to make pool solvency, voting, and claims decisions inspectable across independently governed groups.
disruptionConcepts.coordinationMechanism: Local pools enroll members, collect premiums through a licensed fronting or reciprocal structure, publish common underwriting and claims rules, and federate for reinsurance purchasing, fraud intelligence, and catastrophic risk sharing.
disruptionConcepts.verificationOrTrustModel: Claims are constrained by licensed adjusters, repair invoices, police reports, photos, member review, reserve audits, and cross-pool fraud registries. Governance votes can change rules prospectively, while capital adequacy and reinsurance limits cap underfunded promises.
failureModes: Small pools may suffer adverse selection or one large claim event before reserves mature.
Member voting can become slow, biased, or captured by high-information participants.
Regulatory compliance may force much of the practical operation back into licensed centralized administrators.
adoptionPath: Start with affinity-group supplemental coverage, deductibles, or commercial fleet mutual-aid layers.
Partner with licensed carriers or reinsurers for admitted paper and statutory reserve compliance.
Publish open rating, claims, and governance templates that additional pools can reuse.
Federate pools for reinsurance, fraud detection, and catastrophe backstops once loss experience is credible.

decentralizationFit.rationale: The model decentralizes governance, data rights, and claims oversight while still acknowledging that licensed insurance operations remain necessary.

coordinationCredibility.rationale: Mutual and peer-to-peer insurance mechanics are conceptually credible, but scaling them across U.S. auto insurance requires complex regulatory and capital coordination.

implementationFeasibility.rationale: The technical pieces exist, but legal, actuarial, claims, and reinsurance execution are difficult and jurisdiction-specific.

incumbentPressure.rationale: The concept could pressure transparency, member governance, and niche pricing, but it is unlikely to displace a national incumbent quickly.

products.name: Snapshot
products.summary: Snapshot is Progressive's usage-based insurance program that personalizes auto insurance rates based on driving behavior measured through a mobile app or plug-in device.
products.whyItMatters: Snapshot turns driver behavior into pricing data, making it a strategic bridge between insurance, telematics, consumer surveillance, and risk scoring.
replacementSketch: A replacement would separate driving-data capture from insurer control by using open telematics clients, user-held data vaults, and selective disclosure of signed driving attestations.
Insurers, mutual pools, and brokers could compete to price the same user-controlled risk proofs instead of requiring each carrier to operate a proprietary tracking system.

alternatives.name: Open mileage and driving-risk attestations
alternatives.summary: A protocol pattern using verifiable credentials or signed attestations to let drivers prove mileage, location class, and risk events without handing a proprietary insurer a complete raw telematics history.

openness.rationale: The concept can be built on open verifiable credential standards and interoperable attestations, though no single dominant auto-insurance implementation exists yet.

decentralizationFit.rationale: User-held credentials and selective disclosure move control away from a single insurer-operated telematics silo.

readiness.rationale: The standards are mature enough for pilots, but insurance-grade device integrity, actuarial acceptance, and regulator approval remain unresolved.

costLeverage.rationale: Portable risk proofs could reduce duplicate carrier tracking infrastructure and switching friction, but hardware trust and data validation still carry costs.

disruptionConcepts.name: User-owned telematics risk passport
disruptionConcepts.summary: Drivers could collect driving data in a local or user-controlled vault and disclose only signed risk scores or event proofs to insurers, brokers, or mutual pools, creating portable usage-based insurance without proprietary carrier surveillance.
disruptionConcepts.thesis: Snapshot's strategic value comes from proprietary driving data and carrier-specific pricing integration; a portable risk passport would let drivers shop the same verified behavior across competing insurers and cooperative pools.
disruptionConcepts.bitcoinOrDecentralizationRole: The decentralization role is user custody and selective disclosure of risk data, not Bitcoin settlement. Buyers and insurers coordinate through portable attestations so no single insurer has to own the full raw trip history.
disruptionConcepts.coordinationMechanism: Drivers run approved mobile, vehicle, or OBD clients that sign mileage and driving-event summaries. Insurers publish pricing requirements, drivers authorize limited disclosures, and brokers or marketplaces route proofs to carriers willing to quote.
disruptionConcepts.verificationOrTrustModel: Cheating is constrained through device attestation, sensor cross-checks, odometer consistency, anomaly detection, random audits, cryptographic signatures, and penalties for fraudulent submissions. The model remains weaker where phones can be spoofed or trips can be selectively omitted.
failureModes: Device attestation may exclude older vehicles or privacy-focused devices.
Insurers may refuse portable scores unless they trust the full raw-data pipeline.
Drivers with high-risk behavior may opt out, limiting pool quality and actuarial usefulness.
adoptionPath: Begin with mileage-only and low-mileage discount attestations for privacy-sensitive drivers.
Add signed hard-braking, acceleration, time-of-day, and phone-use summaries once validation is reliable.
Let multiple insurers and mutual pools quote against the same driver-controlled risk passport.
Standardize audit and revocation rules so regulators can evaluate the model.

decentralizationFit.rationale: The concept shifts telematics data custody and disclosure control from the insurer to the driver while enabling multi-party quotation.

coordinationCredibility.rationale: Portable credentials are technically plausible, but the insurance market would need shared data schemas, device-trust rules, and carrier acceptance.

implementationFeasibility.rationale: A pilot could start with mileage or coarse risk proofs, but robust fraud resistance for full usage-based pricing is harder.

incumbentPressure.rationale: If portable risk proofs became accepted, proprietary telematics programs would face pressure to compete on price and service rather than exclusive data capture.
